questions.text: Good vision depends on adequate intake of carotene-rich food. Select the best option from the following state­ments:(1)Vitamin A derivatives are formed from carotene. (2)The photopigments are embedded in the membrane discs of the inner segment. (3)Retinal is a derivative of vitamin A. (4)Retinal is a light-absorbing part of all the visual photopigments.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: 4th option is not related to the statement given above.

Beta carotene is a provitamin A carotenoid, or a nutrient that the body readily converts into vitamin A. Only 1 and 3 are related to the first statement, which emphasizes 'intake' and 'carotene-rich food'; 2 and 4 are not related to intake carotene carotene-rich food.
The 2nd and 4th options are not related to the statement given.
2nd option is not related to the statement above.

questions.explanation: Beta carotene is a provitamin A carotenoid, or a nutrient that the body readily converts into vitamin A. Hence, only 1 and 3 are related to the first statement emphasizes 'intake' and 'carotene-rich food'; 2 and 4 are not related to intake of carotene-rich food.

questions.text: Parts A, B, C and D of the human eye are shown in the diagram.Select the option which gives correct identification along with its functions/characteristics:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: C-­Aqueous chamber - ­Reflects the light which does not pass through the lens
options.explanation: C is aqueous humour. It nourishes the cornea and lens both of which are avascular.

options.text: D- Choroid ­- Its anterior part forms the ciliary body
options.explanation: D is the sclera. It is the outermost covering and maintains the shape of an eyeball. It also protects the inner layers of the eye.

options.text: A - Retina -Contains photoreceptors i.e.rods and cones.
options.explanation: A is the retina which is the innermost layer, containing photoreceptors rods and cones.

options.text: B-Blind spot - ­Has only a few rods and cones.
options.explanation: . B is the blind spot. Optic nerves pierce through the retina at the blind spot. It has no visual cells.

questions.explanation: In the given figure, A is the retina which is the innermost layer, containing photoreceptors rods and cones. B is the blind spot. Optic nerves pierce through the retina at the blind spot. It has no visual cells. C is aqueous humour. It nourishes the cornea and lens both of which are avascular. D is the sclera. It is the outermost covering and maintains the shape of an eyeball. It also protects the inner layers of the eye.

questions.text: A sagittal section of human brain is shown here, Identify at least two labels from A-­D:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: D is cerebellum but C is pons. The cerebellum, located at the back of the brain below the cerebrum. The midbrain is located between the forebrain and hindbrain, forming the upper part of the brain stem. The pons is located in the brainstem, between the midbrain and medulla oblongata.
This is correct. The cerebrum is located at the top and front of the brain, above the brainstem and cerebellum. The pons is located in the brainstem, between the midbrain and medulla oblongata.
Incorrect. B is thalamus and D is cerebellum. The thalamus is located in the center of the brain, above the brainstem. The cerebellum, located at the back of the brain below the cerebrum.

A is cerebral hemisphere but B is thalamus. The cerebral hemispheres are the two large halves of the cerebrum, located on the uppermost part of the brain. The cerebellum, located at the back of the brain below the cerebrum. The thalamus is located in the center of the brain, above the brainstem.

questions.explanation: A is cerebral hemisphere, B is thalamus, C is pons varolii and D is cerebellum. The cerebral hemispheres are the two large halves of the cerebrum, located on the uppermost part of the brain. The thalamus is located in the center of the brain, above the brainstem. The pons is located in the brainstem, between the midbrain and medulla oblongata. The cerebellum is located at the back of the brain, beneath the cerebrum and behind the brainstem.

questions.text: Given below is a diagrammatic cross section of a single loop of human cochlea. Which one of the following options correctly represents the names of three different parts?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: D - Sensory hair cellsA - EndolymphB - Tectorial membrane
options.explanation: This option incorrectly labels the parts. The endolymph is not found where option A suggests.

This option correctly identifies the parts. The perilymph is found in the scala vestibuli and tympani, the tectorial membrane overlays the sensory hair cells, and the endolymph is found in the scala media.

options.text: B - Tectorial membraneC - PerilymphD - Secretory cells
options.explanation: This option incorrectly labels the parts. Secretory cells are not typically labeled in this context, and the perilymph is misidentified.

options.text: C - EndolymphD - Sensory hair cellsA - Serum
options.explanation: This option incorrectly labels the parts. Serum is not present in the cochlea, and the endolymph is misidentified.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct because it accurately labels the components of the cochlea. The perilymph is located in the scala vestibuli and scala tympani, the tectorial membrane is part of the organ of Corti, and the endolymph is present in the scala media. Option A incorrectly identifies the endolymph and perilymph. Option C mislabels secretory cells, which are not typically identified in this context, and misplaces the perilymph. Option D mistakenly includes serum, which is not a component of the cochlear structure.

questions.text: In a man the abducens nerve is injured. Which one of the following functions will be affected?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Abducens is the sixth cranial nerve that innervates the external rectus muscle of the eyeball. It is responsible for turning the eye outwards. The movement of the tongue is controlled by the hypoglossal nerve. Neck movements are controlled by the facial nerve. Swallowing is done by the glossopharyngeal.

The hypoglossal nerve enables tongue movement. It controls the hyoglossus, intrinsic, genioglossus and styloglossusmuscles. These muscles help you speak, swallow and move substances around in your mouth.

The glossopharyngeal nerveends deep inside of your neck near the back of your throat. Among its many functions, it enables swallowing. It's also one of the few nerves in your body with three types of fibers: Motor: Enables muscle movement.
Efferent nerves carry impulses from the spinal cord that cause muscles to contract, controlling cervical movements.

questions.explanation: Abducens is the sixth cranial nerve that innervates the external rectus muscle of the eyeball. It is responsible for turning the eye outwards. The movement of the tongue is controlled by the hypoglossal nerve. Neck movements are controlled by the facial nerve. Swallowing is done by the glossopharyngeal.

questions.text: Hypersecretion of growth hormone in adults does not cause further increase in height because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is accurate. Epiphyseal plates are areas of cartilage at the ends of long bones where growth occurs. They typically close or ossify after adolescence, which means they turn into bone and no longer allow for further increase in height.

options.text: Bones loose their sensitivity to growth hormone in adults
options.explanation: This statement is not entirely accurate. While it's true that the growth plates close after adolescence, it's not because bones lose their sensitivity to growth hormone. Instead, it's due to the natural developmental process.

options.text: Muscle fibres do not grow in size after birth
options.explanation: This statement is generally true. Muscle fibers can grow in size through hypertrophy, but this process is not primarily regulated by growth hormone. It's not directly related to height increase.

options.text: Growth hormone becomes inactive in adults
options.explanation: This statement is not accurate. Growth hormone remains active in adults, although its role shifts from primarily promoting longitudinal bone growth (height) during childhood and adolescence to other functions in adulthood, such as maintaining muscle mass and bone density.

questions.explanation: Epiphyseal plate is a hyaline cartilage plate in the metaphysis at each end of long bone. It is part of long bone where new bone growth takes place until puberty .When the plate ossifies and fuses into the epiphyseal line the growth of bone stops. In adults, elevated levels of GH results in acromegaly where no increase in height occurs because of ossified epiphyseal plate .Instead bones grow in thickness.

questions.text: The posterior pituitary gland is not a ‘true’ endocrine gland because :
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is provided with a duct
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. The posterior lobe of the pituitary gland (neurohypophysis) is a collection of nerve endings and axon terminals from the hypothalamus, not a duct, which stores and releases hormones like oxytocin and antidiuretic hormone (ADH) into the bloodstream.

options.text: It only stores and releases hormones
options.explanation: This statement i correct. The posterior pituitary gland primarily stores and releases hormones produced by the hypothalamus, such as oxytocin and vasopressin (antidiuretic hormone). It does not produce hormones on its own.

options.text: It is under the regulation of hypothalamus
options.explanation: This statement is accurate but it is valid for the whole pituitary gland not just the posterior pituitary . As the whole pituitary gland secretions is controlled by hypothalamus . It is not the reason that why posterior pituitary is not a true endocrine gland.
This statement is incorrect. The posterior pituitary gland does not secrete enzymes; its main function is to store and release hormones.

questions.explanation: The posterior pituitary is not a true endocrine gland because it does not produce its own hormones. Instead, it functions as a storage area for hormones, specifically antidiuretic hormone (ADH) and oxytocin, which are synthesized in the hypothalamus. These hormones are transported down nerve fibers from the hypothalamus to the posterior pituitary, where they are then released into the bloodstream when stimulated.

questions.text: The figure shows the human urinary system with structures labelled A to D. Select the correct option.
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: C­ - Medulla­ - inner zone of the kidney containing complete nephrons
options.explanation: Option A is incorrect because the structure labelled A is not the medulla, but an adrenal gland.

options.text: D ­- Cortex ­- outer part of kidney that does not contain any part of nephrons
options.explanation: Option B is incorrect because the structure labelled D is not the cortex, but part of the urinary system.

options.text: A ­- Adrenal gland ­- located at the anterior part of the kidney. Secretes catecholamines which stimulate glycogen breakdown.
options.explanation: In the given figure, A is an adrenal gland which secretes two catecholamines: adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline (norepinephrine). Adrenaline increases the conversion of glycogen to glucose providing quick energy for a “fight or flight” response. B is the renal pelvis which is a sac-like cavity of the kidney leading to ureters that is not directly connected to the loop of Henle. C is the medulla, the inner region of the kidney containing loop of Henle, collecting ducts and ducts of Bellini. D is the cortex that has proximal and distal convoluted tubules and contains Malpighian corpuscles.

options.text: B ­- Pelvis -­ broad funnel-shaped space inner to the hilum, directly connected to the loops of Henle.
options.explanation: Option D is incorrect because the structure labelled B is not the pelvis, but part of the urinary system.

questions.explanation: In the given figure, A is an adrenal gland which secretes two catecholamines: adrenaline (epinephrine) and noradrenaline (norepinephrine). Adrenaline increases the conversion of glycogen to glucose providing quick energy for a “fight or flight” response. B is the renal pelvis which is a sac-like cavity of the kidney leading to ureters that is not directly connected to the loop of Henle. C is the medulla, the inner region of the kidney containing loop of Henle, collecting ducts and ducts of Bellini. D is the cortex that has proximal and distal convoluted tubules and contains Malpighian corpuscles.

questions.text: A pregnant female delivers a baby who suffers from stunted growth, mental retardation, a low intelligence quotient and abnormal skin. This is the result of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Thyroid cancer typically does not result in the symptoms described in the question. It usually involves a lump or swelling in the neck.
This option is incorrect. The pars distalis is part of the anterior pituitary gland and its oversecretion is not directly related to iodine deficiency or the symptoms described.

This option is correct. Iodine is crucial for the synthesis of thyroid hormones T3 and T4. A deficiency in iodine during pregnancy can lead to a condition known as congenital hypothyroidism in the infant, characterized by stunted growth, mental retardation, and other symptoms.
This option is incorrect. While low secretion of growth hormone can cause dwarfism, it does not account for the mental retardation and other symptoms mentioned in the question.

questions.explanation: Iodine is needed for the synthesis of T3 and T4. Iodine binds to the tyrosine residues in thyroglobulin, which is then hydrolysed into iodotyrosines that combine to form tri-iodothyronine (T3 ) or thyroxine (tetraiodothyronine or T4 ). Therefore, deficiency of iodine in the diet of a pregnant female will lead to the improper synthesis of thyroid hormones in the newly borne infant.

questions.text: Select the option which correctly matches the endocrine gland with its hormone and its function :
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Option A: Thyroid gland - Insulin - Regulates blood sugar levels
options.explanation: The thyroid gland produces hormones like thyroxine, not insulin. Insulin is produced by the pancreas.

options.text: Option B: Adrenal gland - Estrogen - Regulates female reproductive system
options.explanation: The adrenal gland produces hormones like adrenaline and cortisol, not estrogen. Estrogen is primarily produced by the ovaries.

options.text: Option C: Testis - Testosterone - Develops male characteristics
options.explanation: The testis produces testosterone, which is responsible for developing and maintaining male characteristics.

options.text: Option D: Pancreas - Thyroxine - Regulates metabolism
options.explanation: The pancreas produces insulin, not thyroxine. Thyroxine is produced by the thyroid gland.

questions.explanation: Testis are produced by testosterone. The testes are the male reproductive glands that produce sperm for fertilization and the hormone testosterone. Testosterone is a crucial hormone that drives male sexual development, responsible for features like a deepened voice, muscle and body hair growth, and the development of male reproductive organs. It also supports adult male characteristics such as maintaining bone density, muscle mass, and libido.

questions.text: A person entering an empty room suddenly finds a snake right in front of him.Which one of the following is likely to happen in his neuro­hormonal control system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla
options.explanation: The correct answer is: The sympathetic nervous system is activated, releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla—this triggers the “fight-or-flight” response for immediate action.

options.text: Neurotransmitters diffuse rapidly across the cleft and transmit a nerve impulse.
options.explanation: Neurotransmitters diffuse rapidly… – True in general nerve transmission, but not the main event in this sudden danger scenario.

options.text: Hypothalamus activates the parasympathetic division of brain
options.explanation: Hypothalamus activates the parasympathetic division… – Parasympathetic is for “rest-and-digest,” not emergency response.

options.text: Sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal cortex.
options.explanation: Sympathetic nervous system… from the adrenal cortex – Adrenal cortex releases steroid hormones (like cortisol, aldosterone), not epinephrine/norepinephrine.

questions.explanation: Hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine are secreted from the adrenal medulla. They are emergency hormones released in conditions of stress, emergency, etc. Epinephrine and norepinephrine are also released by adrenergic nerve fibres of the sympathetic nervous system, where they act as neurotransmitters.

questions.text: Feeling the tremors of an earthquake a scared resident of the seventh floor of a multistoried building starts climbing down the stairs rapidly. Which hormone initiated this action?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adrenaline, also known as epinephrine, is a key hormone released during stressful situations, often referred to as the 'fight or flight' hormone. It is produced by the adrenal glands and functions to increase heart rate, enhance muscle strength, and elevate breathing rates, thereby preparing the body for immediate physical exertion. In emergency scenarios such as an earthquake, adrenaline is crucial for rapid responses like climbing down stairs quickly.

Glucagon is a hormone that primarily raises blood glucose levels and is released by the pancreas in response to low blood sugar. It plays a role in energy metabolism but is not directly involved in the immediate response to stress or fear.

Gastrin is a hormone that stimulates the secretion of gastric acid in the stomach, aiding digestion. It is not related to the body's stress response or rapid physical actions like those required during an earthquake.

Thyroxine is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland that regulates metabolism. While it is important for maintaining energy levels in the body, it does not play a direct role in the immediate response to stress or emergencies.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Adrenaline. This hormone is crucial during stressful situations, such as feeling the tremors of an earthquake, as it prepares the body for rapid physical action, which is essential for survival. Adrenaline enhances heart rate, increases blood flow to muscles, and facilitates quicker responses, which would enable a resident to climb down stairs rapidly.In contrast, Glucagon, Gastrin, and Thyroxine do not have immediate effects on the physical responses associated with stress. Glucagon is primarily involved in blood sugar regulation, Gastrin aids in digestion, and Thyroxine controls metabolic rate, making them irrelevant to the scenario described in the question.

questions.text: Sarin is a potent organophosphate that can be used in chemical warfare. As an inhibitor of acetylcholinesterase, sarin causes excessive buildup of acetylcholine in all synapses where it is the neurotransmitter. Which of the following symptoms would most likely be seen in an individual with sarin poisoning?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Increased urination occurs due to enhanced bladder stimulation, while increased sweating results from the activation of sweat glands by accumulated acetylcholine.
While sarin poisoning does cause increased urination due to bladder stimulation, it also leads to increased sweating, not decreased, due to excessive acetylcholine.
Sarin poisoning typically results in increased urination and sweating due to overstimulation of the parasympathetic nervous system, not decreased urination.
This option is incorrect because sarin leads to increased glandular activity, not decreased, due to the buildup of acetylcholine.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is: Increased urination and increased sweating. Sarin inhibits acetylcholinesterase, causing acetylcholine accumulation in the synapses. This stimulates the parasympathetic nervous system, leading to increased glandular secretions, such as urination (diuresis) and sweating (diaphoresis). The bladder contracts more frequently, and sweat glands are activated.Option B is incorrect because sweating increases, not decreases, with sarin poisoning. Option C incorrectly suggests decreased urination. Option D is incorrect because both urination and sweating are not decreased but increased.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of negative feedback?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Childbirth contractions are a classic example of positive feedback, where the body amplifies the signal until the baby is born, rather than restoring balance.
Blood clotting is primarily a positive feedback mechanism that enhances the clotting process until the bleeding stops. It does not serve to restore balance in the body.

Thermoregulation is an example of negative feedback, where the body regulates its temperature by initiating responses to counteract deviations from the normal range, such as sweating to cool down or shivering to generate heat.
Action potential involves a rapid change in membrane potential that is not a feedback mechanism aimed at restoring balance; instead, it is a process that leads to the transmission of a nerve signal.

questions.explanation: Thermoregulation is an example of negative feedback because it maintains a stable body temperature. When body temperature increases, sweating and vasodilation cool the body. When temperature decreases, shivering and vasoconstriction warm the body back to normal.

questions.text: Nissl’s granules are found in the cyton of nerve cells. They have an affinity for basic dyes. The granules are made up of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mitochondria are known as the powerhouse of the cell, generating energy in the form of ATP, but they are not involved in Nissl's granules.
Cell metabolites are products of metabolism that can serve various functions, such as storage or signaling, but they do not constitute Nissl's granules.
Fat granules store energy in the form of lipids, but they are not related to Nissl's granules, which are involved in protein synthesis.
Nissl's granules consist of rough endoplasmic reticulum and clusters of ribosomes, which are essential for protein synthesis in nerve cells.

questions.explanation: Nissl's granules are prominently found in the cyton (cell body) of nerve cells, where they are involved in protein synthesis. They consist of rough endoplasmic reticulum and clusters of free ribosomes, which give them an affinity for basic dyes. This composition is crucial for the synthesis of proteins necessary for the neuron's functions.Why other options are incorrect:Mitochondria: While critical for energy production, they do not form Nissl's granules.Cell Metabolites: These are not structural components of Nissl's granules.Fat Granules: These are involved in energy storage, not protein synthesis.

questions.text: Imagination and creativity are generally associated with
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The right cerebral hemisphere is involved with visual and spatial processing, and is commonly linked to creativity, imagination, and artistic abilities.
The left hemisphere is associated with logical reasoning, analytical processing, language, and mathematical skills, rather than creativity and imagination.
The temporal lobe is primarily associated with processing sensory input into derived meanings, emotional responses, and memory formation, not directly with creativity and imagination.
The occipital lobe is mainly responsible for visual processing and does not play a direct role in creativity or imaginative thinking.

questions.explanation: The right cerebral hemisphere of the human brain includes areas associated with abstraction, artistic ability, and emotional response, making it the center for creativity and imagination. In contrast, the left hemisphere is more involved in logical and analytical processes such as language and mathematics. The temporal lobe deals with sensory processing and memory, while the occipital lobe is primarily concerned with visual information. Therefore, the right cerebral hemisphere is the most suitable answer when considering creativity and imagination.

questions.text: Spot out the parasympathetic effect from the following
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The parasympathetic nervous system typically reduces blood pressure by dilating blood vessels, not increasing it.
Dilation of pupils is associated with the sympathetic nervous system's 'fight or flight' response. The parasympathetic nervous system actually constricts the pupils.
The parasympathetic nervous system stimulates the secretion of various digestive juices, aiding in digestion, which is a key 'rest and digest' function.
The parasympathetic nervous system reduces cardiac output by decreasing heart rate and force of contraction, contrary to increasing it.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is 'Secretion of digestive juices'. The parasympathetic nervous system is responsible for stimulating activities that occur when the body is at rest, including the secretion of digestive juices such as saliva, gastric juice, and bile. This supports the body's 'rest and digest' functions. In contrast, the sympathetic nervous system prepares the body for 'fight or flight' responses and is responsible for actions like increasing blood pressure and dilating pupils. The parasympathetic nervous system decreases cardiac output by reducing heart rate, rather than increasing it.

questions.text: In the central nervous system, myelinated parts of the neuron form the _, while the non-myelinated parts of the cells form the _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Grey matter is composed of non-myelinated neurons, primarily cell bodies, giving it a greyish appearance. White matter consists of myelinated axons.
This option is correct. Myelinated parts of neurons contribute to the white appearance of white matter, while the non-myelinated parts form grey matter.
This option is incorrect. There is no yellow matter in the central nervous system. White matter is made of myelinated axons.
This option is incorrect. There is no red or yellow matter in the central nervous system.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: White matter, grey matter. In the central nervous system, myelinated axons form the white matter. The myelin sheath gives these parts a white appearance. In contrast, grey matter is composed of non-myelinated cell bodies and dendrites, giving it a greyish appearance.Option A is incorrect because it reverses the characteristics of grey and white matter. Options C and D are incorrect because 'yellow matter' and 'red matter' do not exist in the central nervous system.

questions.text: Animals possess a nerve network or nervous system to respond to their environment; but the single-cell Amoeba does not possess any nerve cell. How does it come to know whether a particle it encounters is a grain of sand and not its food?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Amoebas respond to chemical stimuli through chemotaxis, allowing them to differentiate between food and non-food particles based on chemical signals.
An Amoeba does not have skin or nerve cells that can detect particles; it relies solely on chemical signals for such determinations.
Hormones are not involved in the detection of particles by Amoebas; they use chemotaxis for this purpose.
This is incorrect as only chemotaxis is the mechanism used by Amoebas to identify particles.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that an Amoeba uses chemotaxis to determine whether a particle is food or not. Chemotaxis is a process by which an organism moves towards or away from chemical stimuli. In the case of an Amoeba, it detects chemical signals from potential food sources, enabling it to distinguish between edible and non-edible particles. The other options are incorrect because Amoebas do not possess skin or hormones to detect particles, nor do they use multiple mechanisms for this purpose.

questions.text: The opening at the base of the skull for spinal cord is called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The foramen magnum is the large opening at the base of the skull through which the spinal cord passes and connects to the brain.
The foramen of Monro is a small conduit in the brain that connects the lateral ventricles to the third ventricle, unrelated to the spinal cord.
The obturator foramen is located in the pelvis, not the skull, and is not involved in the connection between the brain and spinal cord.

Also known as the median aperture, the foramen of Magendie is part of the ventricular system in the brain, linking the fourth ventricle and the cisterna magna, and does not relate to the spinal cord exit.

questions.explanation: The foramen magnum is the correct answer as it is the opening at the base of the skull that allows the spinal cord to connect with the brain. This connection is essential for the coordination and control of body functions. The other foramen options are located in different parts of the body or serve different functions. The foramen of Monro connects brain ventricles, the obturator foramen is in the pelvis, and the foramen of Magendie is part of the brain's ventricular system.

questions.text: The largest number of cell bodies of neurons in a human body is found in the —---
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brain is the central processing unit of the nervous system, containing the highest concentration of neurons and their cell bodies, also known as grey matter.
The spinal cord contains neuron cell bodies but is primarily responsible for transmitting signals between the brain and the rest of the body. It does not have as many neurons as the brain.
The retina is part of the eye and contains photoreceptor cells, not a large number of neuron cell bodies, unlike the brain.
The tongue has sensory neurons but lacks a significant concentration of neuron cell bodies compared to the brain.

questions.explanation: The brain is the major center of coordination and control in the human body, integrating sensory information and issuing commands. It contains the largest number of neuron cell bodies, known as grey matter, which is crucial for processing complex information. In contrast, the spinal cord serves as a communication pathway between the brain and the peripheral nerves, with fewer neuron cell bodies. The retina is specialized for detecting light, and the tongue primarily serves in taste sensation, neither of which have the extensive network of neuron cell bodies found in the brain.

questions.text: Which part of the brain is more developed in humans
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The medulla oblongata is located at the base of the brainstem and is responsible for autonomic functions such as breathing and heart rate. It is not the most developed part of the brain in terms of higher cognitive functions.
The cerebellum, situated at the back of the brain, coordinates voluntary movements and balance. Although important, it is not the most developed region in humans for complex thought processes.

The cerebrum is the largest and most developed part of the human brain, responsible for higher cognitive functions such as reasoning, decision-making, and problem-solving. Its extensive surface area due to the cerebral cortex allows for advanced processing.
The occipital lobe is primarily involved in visual processing and is located at the back of the brain. While crucial for vision, it is not the most developed part of the human brain overall.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the cerebrum. It is the largest and most developed part of the brain, accounting for approximately 85% of its weight. The cerebrum is responsible for higher brain functions such as thought, action, and coordination of movement. It is divided into two hemispheres and further into four lobes, each with specialized functions. The extensive surface area of the cerebral cortex is key to the complex cognitive abilities of humans.The other options are incorrect because the medulla is involved in autonomic functions, the cerebellum manages coordination and balance, and the occipital lobe is primarily focused on visual processing.

questions.text: This cell which is the longest in the human body:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Muscle cells, also known as muscle fibers, are responsible for the contraction and movement of muscles in the body. While muscle cells can vary in length depending on the muscle they belong to, they are generally not as long as nerve cells. Muscle fibers are elongated cells that can span from a few millimeters to several centimeters in length. They contain specialized structures called myofibrils, which are responsible for the contractile properties of muscle tissue.

Nerve cells, or neurons, are specialized cells of the nervous system that transmit electrical signals throughout the body. Neurons consist of a cell body, dendrites (short, branched extensions that receive signals from other neurons), and an elongated projection called an axon. It is the axon that can be exceptionally long, allowing for communication over long distances. For example, some neurons in the spinal cord extend all the way to the toes or fingertips, making them the longest cells in the human body.

Bone cells, also known as osteocytes, are the primary cells found in bone tissue. While bone cells play a vital role in maintaining bone health and remodeling, they are not typically characterized by their length. Bone tissue itself can be quite long, as it forms the structural framework of the skeletal system, but individual bone cells do not exhibit significant elongation compared to nerve cells.

Gland cells are found in various glands of the body and are responsible for producing and secreting substances such as hormones, enzymes, or mucus. Gland cells can have different shapes and sizes depending on the gland they belong to, but they are generally not known for their exceptional length. Their function primarily involves the production and release of specific substances rather than elongated structures.

questions.explanation: The nerve cells or neurons are the longest cells in the human body, with some neurons being as long as 1-1.5 meters. These cells are responsible for transmitting signals throughout the body. Neurons consist of a cell body, dendrites (short, branched extensions that receive signals from other neurons), and an elongated projection called an axon. It is the axon that can be exceptionally long, allowing for communication over long distances. For example, some neurons in the spinal cord extend all the way to the toes or fingertips, making them the longest cells in the human body.

questions.text: One of the actions of the parasympathetic nervous system is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Peristalsis is the wave-like muscular contractions that occur in the digestive tract, helping to move food along the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. The parasympathetic nervous system promotes peristalsis, enhancing digestive functions, rather than inhibiting them.

Sweat secretion is controlled by the sympathetic nervous system, not the parasympathetic nervous system. The sympathetic nervous system is responsible for activating the sweat glands, leading to increased sweating during times of stress or heightened activity.

This is the correct answer. When the parasympathetic nervous system is activated, it leads to the constriction of the pupils (miosis). Conversely, the sympathetic nervous system causes pupil dilation (mydriasis).

Bronchiole dilation (bronchodilation) is primarily controlled by the sympathetic nervous system. When the sympathetic system is activated, it causes relaxation of the smooth muscles in the bronchioles, leading to increased airflow and improved breathing.

questions.explanation: In summary, the correct answer is "c) Constriction of Pupils." The parasympathetic nervous system is responsible for causing pupil constriction, while the sympathetic nervous system controls pupil dilation.

questions.text: Information received by different receptors of body is analyzed by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The Autonomic nervous system controls involuntary functions and connects the central nervous system to visceral organs. It is not primarily responsible for analyzing sensory information.

The Central nervous system, composed of the brain and spinal cord, is responsible for processing and analyzing information received from sensory receptors, controlling both voluntary and involuntary activities.
The Parasympathetic nervous system is part of the Autonomic nervous system, focusing on resting states and conserving energy. It does not analyze sensory information.
The Sympathetic nervous system, another part of the Autonomic nervous system, prepares the body for action in emergencies. It is involved in the fight-or-flight response, not sensory analysis.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.The Central nervous system (CNS) is primarily responsible for analyzing information received by receptors throughout the body. It includes the brain and spinal cord, which integrate sensory data and coordinate responses. The Autonomic nervous system, which includes both the Sympathetic and Parasympathetic nervous systems, controls involuntary bodily functions and does not analyze sensory information. Therefore, the CNS is the correct answer as it processes and interprets sensory input.

questions.text: Ectoderm mainly gives rise to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The musculoskeletal system primarily arises from the mesoderm. The mesoderm differentiates into cells that form bones, muscles, and connective tissues.
The ectoderm is the germ layer that primarily gives rise to the nervous system, including the brain, spinal cord, and peripheral nerves.
The respiratory system is mainly derived from the endoderm, which forms the lining of the respiratory tract.
The cardiovascular system develops from the mesoderm, which forms the heart, blood vessels, and blood cells.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.The ectoderm is one of the three primary germ layers in the early embryo and primarily gives rise to the nervous system and the epidermis of the skin. The nervous system includes the brain, spinal cord, and nerves. The other options are incorrect: the musculoskeletal and cardiovascular systems develop from the mesoderm, while the respiratory system originates from the endoderm.

questions.text: Stimulus of touch, pain, cold and heat are recognized differently because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: For each sensation, different form of nerve impulse is generated
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Nerve impulses, or action potentials, are electrical signals that are generated and transmitted by neurons to convey information. While the intensity and frequency of nerve impulses can vary based on the strength and duration of a stimulus, the basic form of the nerve impulse itself remains the same regardless of the type of sensation.

options.text: Different receptors are present for detection of stimuli
options.explanation: The dermis of the skin contains receptors for touch, pressure, temperature and pain. Meissner's corpuscles and Merkel disks for touch. Paccinian corpuscles for pressure. Mechanoreceptor for touch and pressure both and Thermoreceptors for change in temperature of body.

options.text: All nerves send their impulse to same part of brain
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Nerves carrying sensory information from different sensory receptors do not all send their impulses to the same part of the brain. Instead, sensory nerves project to specific regions of the brain that are dedicated to processing and interpreting different types of sensory information. For example, touch sensations are processed in somatosensory areas of the brain, while temperature sensations are processed in specific thermoreceptive regions.

options.text: Each stimulus acts on different parts of body
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. While different stimuli may elicit sensations in different parts of the body, it is not the explanation for why the sensations of touch, pain, cold, and heat are recognized differently. The differentiation in sensory perception arises from the different types of sensory receptors and the neural pathways through which the signals are transmitted to the brain.

questions.explanation: Stimuli are always detected by specific receptors present in different parts of the body. Stimulus of touch, pain, cold and heat are recognized differently because these stimuli are detected by different receptors present in different parts of the body.Human receptors are categorized by the stimulus they detect (mechanoreceptors, chemoreceptors, thermoreceptors, photoreceptors, nociceptors) or by their location and function (proprioceptors, interoceptors, exteroceptors). Additionally, cell-surface receptors, crucial for cell communication, are classified by their signal-transducing mechanism, including G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), ion channel-linked receptors, and enzyme-linked receptors.

questions.text: Which one of the following are also known as efferent neurons?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons are neurons that carry signals from the sensory receptors to the CNS. Sensory neurons are responsible for transmitting information about the external and internal environment to the brain. They are also known as afferent neurons.

Associative neurons are neurons that connect sensory and motor neurons within the CNS. Associative neurons are responsible for processing information and integrating it into motor responses. They are also known as inter or relay neuron.

Motor neurons are also known as efferent neurons. Efferent neurons are neurons that carry signals away from the central nervous system (CNS) to the muscles and glands. Motor neurons are responsible for initiating and controlling voluntary movement.
Mixed neurons are neurons that have both sensory and motor axons. Mixed neurons are found in the autonomic nervous system, which controls involuntary functions such as heart rate and digestion.

questions.explanation: Motor neurons are also known as efferent neurons. Efferent neurons are neurons that carry signals away from the central nervous system (CNS) to the muscles and glands. Motor neurons are responsible for initiating and controlling voluntary movement. Motor neurons are specialized nerve cells that send commands from the brain and spinal cord to muscles and glands, enabling both voluntary and involuntary movements, such as walking, speaking, and swallowing. There are two main types: upper motor neurons (originating in the brain) and lower motor neurons (originating in the spinal cord and brainstem). These neurons are crucial for controlling bodily functions and are the targets of several neurodegenerative diseases, including Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS).

questions.text: Depolarization is the result of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The influx of potassium ions (K+) into a cell does not lead to depolarization. It can have the opposite effect, contributing to repolarization or hyperpolarization.

Depolarization is primarily the result of an influx of sodium ions (Na+). This influx of positively charged sodium ions into a cell, particularly a neuron, reduces the cell's membrane potential, making it less negative. This change in membrane potential is a key step in the initiation of an action potential.
Efflux of potassium ions (K+) can contribute to repolarization, as potassium ions leaving the cell make the membrane potential more negative, bringing it closer to its resting state.
Efflux of sodium ions (Na+) would not contribute to depolarization. Sodium ions leaving the cell would tend to restore the resting membrane potential.

questions.explanation: During depolarization, the neuron’s voltage-gated sodium (Na⁺) channels open in response to a stimulus.Na⁺ ions then flow into the cell because their concentration is higher outside and the inside is negatively charged.This influx of positive ions causes the membrane potential to rise from about –70 mV (resting potential) toward +30 mV, making the inside less negative and more positive. This is the depolarization phase of the action potential.

questions.text: Which of the following diagram best shows the resting membrane potential?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Not correct because during resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside.
Correct because during resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside.
Not correct because during resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside, not half positive or half negative.
Not correct because during resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside, not half positive or half negative.

questions.explanation: During resting membrane potential, membrane will be highly positive from outside and highly negative from inside due to the concentration of K+ and Na+ ions. The resting membrane potential (RMP) is the electrical potential difference across a cell's plasma membrane when the cell is in a non-excited state. This negative charge inside the cell, typically ranging from −70 mV to −90 mV for neurons, is maintained by the sodium-potassium pump, leakage of potassium ions out of the cell, and the presence of large negative anions within the cell. The RMP allows cells, especially neurons, to be ready to transmit signals when stimulated.

questions.text: Which of the following neurotransmitters affect sleep, mood and learning?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While dopamine is involved in mood and learning, epinephrine primarily regulates metabolism, attention, and the body's 'fight-or-flight' response, rather than sleep or mood.
Serotonin does affect mood and sleep, but norepinephrine is more related to alertness and stress responses rather than directly influencing mood or learning.

Both dopamine and serotonin are crucial for mood regulation, sleep, and learning. Serotonin helps produce melatonin, which affects the sleep-wake cycle, while dopamine is critical for learning and mood stabilization.
Both are mainly involved in the body's immediate stress response and do not directly influence mood, sleep, or learning in the same way as serotonin and dopamine.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Dopamine and serotonin. Both neurotransmitters play a significant role in mood regulation, the sleep-wake cycle, and learning processes. Dopamine is essential for learning and motivation, while serotonin is involved in mood stabilization and sleep regulation through melatonin production. The other options include neurotransmitters like epinephrine and norepinephrine, which are more associated with stress responses and alertness rather than directly influencing mood, sleep, or learning.

questions.text: Hearing is concerned with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cerebral cortex is responsible for processing sensory information, including auditory signals. It contains the primary auditory cortex, which is crucial for interpreting sounds.
The medulla oblongata controls autonomic functions like breathing and heart rate, but it is not involved in processing auditory information.
The hypothalamus regulates hormonal functions and homeostasis but does not process auditory information.
The cerebellum is involved in coordinating movement and balance, not in processing sounds.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the Cerebral Cortex, which contains the primary auditory cortex responsible for processing and interpreting sounds. The other options are incorrect because the medulla, hypothalamus, and cerebellum serve different functions unrelated to interpreting auditory signals: the medulla controls autonomic functions, the hypothalamus regulates hormonal and homeostatic activities, and the cerebellum manages motor control and balance.

questions.text: Gray matter of spinal cord contains:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: No, ganglia are not part of the gray matter in the spinal cord. Ganglia are collections of nerve cell bodies located outside the central nervous system.
Incorrect. Myelinated cell processes are characteristic of white matter, not gray matter, which contains unmyelinated cell processes along with cell bodies.

options.text: Cell bodies and unmyelinated cell processes
options.explanation: Correct. Gray matter in the spinal cord is composed of neuron cell bodies and unmyelinated cell processes, which are crucial for processing and transmitting nerve signals.

options.text: Cell bodies and myelinated cell processes
options.explanation: No, this is incorrect. While gray matter contains cell bodies, myelinated cell processes are part of the white matter.

questions.explanation: Option C is correct. The gray matter of the spinal cord consists of neuron cell bodies and unmyelinated cell processes, including dendrites and axons that are involved in local processing of information. This area is responsible for integrating sensory information and coordinating motor function. In contrast, myelinated cell processes, which facilitate faster signal transmission, are found in the white matter. Therefore, Options A, B, and D are incorrect as they either include ganglia, which are not part of the spinal cord, or incorrectly associate myelinated processes with gray matter.

questions.text: Which would receive information from a sensory neuron?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brain, specifically the thalamus, acts as a central processing unit. It receives and processes information from sensory neurons before relaying it to appropriate brain regions for further interpretation. This makes the brain the correct answer.

Glands do not directly receive information from sensory neurons. Instead, they produce and secrete hormones or other substances, often in response to signals from the brain or other parts of the endocrine system.
Muscles respond to motor neuron signals, not sensory neurons. They are involved in movement and posture control, but do not directly process sensory information.

Receptors detect stimuli and convert them into electrical signals which are then sent to sensory neurons. They do not receive information from sensory neurons; instead, they initiate the sensory pathway.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the brain. Sensory neurons carry information from receptors throughout the body to the brain for processing. The thalamus, a part of the brain, acts as a relay center, receiving these impulses and directing them to appropriate areas for interpretation and response. Other options like glands, muscles, and receptors do not receive information from sensory neurons. Glands produce secretions, muscles execute movements, and receptors detect stimuli and send information to sensory neurons.

questions.text: Which is most typical cause of nerve deafness?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: It is caused by damage to the eardrum or ossicles
options.explanation: This describes conductive hearing loss, not nerve deafness. Damage to the eardrum or ossicles affects the transmission of sound waves through the middle ear, not the inner ear or auditory nerve.

options.text: It is caused by damage to the hair cells in the cochlea
options.explanation: This describes nerve deafness, also known as sensorineural hearing loss. It occurs when the hair cells in the cochlea are damaged, affecting the ability to send accurate nerve signals to the brain.

options.text: It is more common in younger people
options.explanation: Nerve deafness is not more common in younger people; it is often associated with aging or exposure to loud noise over time.

options.text: It results in loss of balance and coordination
options.explanation: While inner ear issues can affect balance, nerve deafness specifically refers to hearing loss, not balance and coordination issues.

questions.explanation: Nerve deafness is a permanent hearing loss that occurs when there is damage to either the tiny hair-like cells of the inner ear(cochlea), known as stereocilia, or the auditory nerve itself, which prevents or weakens the transfer of nerve signals to the brain.

questions.text: Given above is a diagrammatic cross section of a single loop of human cochlea. Which one of the following options correctly represents the names of three different parts?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: D: Sensory hair cells, A: Endolymph, B: Tectorial membrane, C: Perilymph
options.explanation: This answer does not correspond to the correct labelling

options.text: A: Perilymph, B: Tectorial membrane, C: Endolymph, D: Sensory hair cells
options.explanation: Cochlea is the main hearing organ, where sound waves are transformed into electrical impulses which are sent on to the brain. It consists of three fluid-filled chambers or canals, the upper scala vestibuli, lower scala tympani and middle scala media.Both scala vestibuli and scala tympani are filled with perilymph, while scala media is filled with endolymph. The tectorial membrane overhangs the sensory hair in scala media.

options.text: B: Tectorial membrane, C: Perilymph, D: Secretory cells, A: Endolymph
options.explanation: This answer does not correspond to the correct labelling

options.text: C: Endolymph, D: Sensory hair cells, A: Serum, B: Secretory cells
options.explanation: This answer does not correspond to the correct labelling

questions.explanation: Cochlea is the main hearing organ, where sound waves are transformed into electrical impulses which are sent on to the brain. It consists of three fluid-filled chambers or canals, the upper scala vestibuli, lower scala tympani and middle scala media.Both scala vestibuli and scala tympani are filled with perilymph, while scala media is filled with endolymph. The tectorial membrane overhangs the sensory hair in scala media.

questions.text: Which statement is correct regarding to the events take place during the action potential at the axon;
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Sodium and potassium ions reach an equilibrium across the membrane during an action potential
options.explanation: This is incorrect as sodium and potassium ions do not reach equilibrium during an action potential. Instead, specific ion gradients are actively maintained, primarily by the sodium-potassium pump, which pumps three sodium ions out for every two potassium ions brought in.

options.text: During the peak of the action potential, sodium ion concentration inside the axon is higher than potassium ion concentration outside
options.explanation: This is correct. At the peak of an action potential, sodium ions have rushed into the axon, raising their concentration inside the axon above the potassium ion concentration outside, causing depolarization.

options.text: There are more potassium ions inside than outside the axon at rest, but this does not describe the action potential
options.explanation: This statement is true at rest, but does not explain the ion movements during an action potential. During an action potential, potassium ions flow out of the cell, altering this balance temporarily.

options.text: The concentration of sodium and potassium ions remains constant during an action potential
options.explanation: This statement is false. The concentrations of sodium and potassium ions change significantly during an action potential, which is essential for the generation and propagation of nerve impulses.

questions.explanation: During an action potential, the rapid influx of sodium ions into the axon causes the membrane to depolarize, reaching a peak when the sodium ion concentration inside the cell becomes higher than the potassium ion concentration outside. This depolarization is followed by the efflux of potassium ions, which leads to repolarization of the membrane. Hence, the correct statement is that during the peak of the action potential, the sodium ion concentration inside the axon is higher than the potassium ion concentration outside. Option A is incorrect as it assumes equilibrium, which does not occur during an action potential. Option C, while true at rest, does not pertain to the action potential itself. Option D is false because ion concentrations are indeed disturbed during this process.

questions.text: The thalamus and the hypothalamus are located in which region of the brain?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brain stem is a part of the hindbrain, whereas the thalamus and hypothalamus are parts of the forebrain. Hence, this option is incorrect.
The cerebrum is a part of the forebrain, along with the thalamus and hypothalamus. Hence, this option is incorrect.
The cerebellum is a part of the hindbrain, whereas the thalamus and hypothalamus are parts of the forebrain. Hence, this option is incorrect.
The forebrain is further divided into the diencephalon and telencephalon. The diencephalon consists of the thalamus and hypothalamus. Hence, this option is the correct one.

questions.explanation: The forebrain is further divided into the diencephalon and telencephalon. The diencephalon consists of the thalamus and hypothalamus. Hence, this option is the correct one.

questions.text: You want to train your puppy to wait at the curb until you tell him to cross the road. Your friend advises you to give your dog a treat every time he does as you ask. Your friend is advising that you train the dog using
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Operant conditioning involves learning through the consequences of voluntary behavior, using rewards or punishments to increase or decrease the likelihood of a behavior. In this scenario, giving a treat to the puppy for waiting at the curb is a reward, reinforcing the desired behavior.

Classical conditioning involves learning through association between a neutral stimulus and an unconditioned stimulus, leading to a conditioned response. This does not involve rewards or voluntary behaviors, so it does not apply to the scenario described.

Imprinting is a rapid learning process occurring at a particular life stage, forming attachments and developing preferences. It is not related to voluntary behavior modification through rewards, making it irrelevant here.

Habituation involves a decrease in response to a repeated stimulus, allowing animals to ignore irrelevant stimuli. It does not involve learning through rewards or punishments, so it is not applicable in this context.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Operant conditioning. This form of learning involves modifying behavior through consequences, such as rewards or punishments. In the scenario, giving the puppy a treat every time he waits at the curb is a positive reinforcement, encouraging the desired behavior. The other options are not suitable: Classical conditioning involves learning through association, not rewards; Imprinting is about forming early life attachments and does not involve voluntary behavior changes; and Habituation is about reduced responses to repeated stimuli, not behavior modification through incentives.

questions.text: An animal that sacrifices itself for its relatives is exhibiting
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Operant conditioning, sometimes referred to as instrumental conditioning, is a method of learning that uses rewards and punishment to modify behavior. Through operant conditioning, behavior that is rewarded is likely to be repeated, and behavior that is punished will rarely occur.

In kin selection, an individual sacrifices itself for the family, a group that shares its genes. Perhaps the individual that sacrificed itself will not get to pass on its genes, but its relatives will.
Classical conditioning theory states that behaviors are learned by connecting a neutral stimulus with a positive one. The learned behavior is called a conditioned response.

Imprinting refers to a well-researched phenomenon within animals where they form an extremely close and dependent bond with the first animal they see after being born. For example, when ducklings hatch, they tend to imprint on their parent and follow them everywhere.

questions.explanation: In kin selection, an individual sacrifices itself for the family, a group that shares its genes. Perhaps the individual that sacrificed itself will not get to pass on its genes, but its relatives will.

questions.text: The main function of abscisic acid in the plant is to
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Abscisic acid does not function to increase cell length; rather, it inhibits growth activities. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Abscisic acid is not involved in promoting cell division; it primarily acts as a growth inhibitor. Hence, this option is incorrect.
Abscisic acid is known as a growth inhibitor hormone. It helps plants cope with stress by inhibiting growth and promoting dormancy, making this the correct option.
Abscisic acid does not promote the growth of the stem; it inhibits growth to manage stress conditions. Hence, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Abscisic acid is primarily known for its role as a growth inhibitor in plants. It helps in managing stress by inhibiting growth and inducing dormancy, especially during unfavorable conditions. This hormone also triggers the closing of stomata to prevent water loss through transpiration. The other options incorrectly attribute growth-promoting roles to abscisic acid, which are functions typically associated with other hormones like auxins or gibberellins.

questions.text: Tropic hormones
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is incorrect. The thyroid gland produces hormones like thyroxine and calcitonin, which regulate metabolism and calcium levels, but it does not produce tropic hormones.
This is incorrect. The liver is primarily involved in detoxification and metabolism, not the secretion of tropic hormones.
This is incorrect. The hypothalamus releases hormones that control the pituitary gland, but it does not directly secrete tropic hormones.

options.text: Are hormones that stimulate other glands
options.explanation: This is correct. Tropic hormones are hormones that stimulate other endocrine glands to release their hormones, and they are primarily secreted by the anterior pituitary gland.

questions.explanation: Tropic hormones are crucial in the endocrine system as they stimulate other endocrine glands to produce and release their hormones. They are predominantly secreted by the anterior pituitary gland. This regulatory mechanism is essential for maintaining hormonal balance and coordinating various physiological processes. The thyroid and liver do not produce tropic hormones. While the hypothalamus does influence the pituitary gland's release of tropic hormones, it does not secrete them directly.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about receptors?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Gustatory receptors detect taste while olfactory receptors detect smell.
options.explanation: This statement is accurate. Gustatory receptors on the tongue are responsible for detecting taste, while olfactory receptors in the nasal cavity detect odors.

options.text: Both gustatory and olfactory receptors detect smell.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Gustatory receptors are involved in taste detection, not smell.

options.text: Auditory receptors detect smell and olfactory receptors detect taste.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Auditory receptors are involved in hearing, and olfactory receptors are involved in detecting smells.

options.text: Olfactory receptors detect taste and gustatory receptors smell.
options.explanation: This is incorrect. Olfactory receptors detect smells and gustatory receptors detect tastes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is that gustatory receptors detect taste while olfactory receptors detect smell. Gustatory receptors are found in the taste buds on the tongue and are responsible for detecting different flavors. Olfactory receptors are located in the nasal cavity and detect airborne molecules associated with odors. The other options confuse the functions of these receptors, mixing up their specific roles or incorrectly assigning them to unrelated sensory functions such as hearing.

questions.text: Excess MSH is secreted in
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Addison’s disease often leads to hyperpigmentation due to excess secretion of MSH, as the pituitary gland increases production of ACTH which can cross-react with MSH receptors.
Alzheimer’s disease is related to the buildup of amyloid plaques in the brain and does not involve the secretion of MSH.
Parkinson’s disease involves the degeneration of neurons that produce dopamine. It is not related to MSH secretion.
Grave’s disease causes an overproduction of thyroid hormones due to autoimmune activity, not related to MSH secretion.

questions.explanation: Excess MSH (Melanocyte Stimulating Hormone) is secreted in Addison’s disease due to increased production of ACTH by the pituitary gland, which can lead to hyperpigmentation. In Addison’s disease, the adrenal glands produce insufficient cortisol, prompting the pituitary gland to secrete more ACTH, which can also increase MSH levels. This is not the case in Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, or Grave’s disease. Alzheimer’s involves amyloid plaques, Parkinson’s involves dopamine deficiency, and Grave’s involves thyroid hormone overproduction, none of which are related to MSH secretion.

questions.text: The diagram shows a section through the brain and spinal cord. Which of the labelled parts of the diagram secretes hormones?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The pituitary gland, labeled as A, is known as the 'master' gland because it secretes hormones that regulate other glands in the endocrine system. It is situated at the base of the brain.
This part of the brain is involved in processes other than hormone secretion, such as controlling movement or sensory processing.
This region of the brain is primarily associated with processing sensory information and does not secrete hormones.
This area is involved in coordinating voluntary movements and does not have a role in hormone secretion.

questions.explanation: The pituitary gland, labeled as A, is crucial for hormone secretion and is often referred to as the 'master' gland due to its role in controlling other endocrine glands. It secretes various hormones that regulate growth, metabolism, and reproduction. The other options, B, C, and D, correspond to brain regions that do not participate in hormone secretion but are involved in other vital functions such as movement coordination and sensory processing.

questions.text: A person entering an empty room suddenly finds a snake right in front of him.Which one of the following is likely to happen in his neuro­hormonal control system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla
options.explanation: The correct answer is: The sympathetic nervous system is activated, releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal medulla—this triggers the “fight-or-flight” response for immediate action.

options.text: Neurotransmitters diffuse rapidly across the cleft and transmit a nerve impulse.
options.explanation: Neurotransmitters diffuse rapidly… – True in general nerve transmission, but not the main event in this sudden danger scenario.

options.text: Hypothalamus activates the parasympathetic division of brain
options.explanation: Hypothalamus activates the parasympathetic division… – Parasympathetic is for “rest-and-digest,” not emergency response.

options.text: Sympathetic nervous system is activated releasing epinephrine and norepinephrine from the adrenal cortex.
options.explanation: Sympathetic nervous system… from the adrenal cortex – Adrenal cortex releases steroid hormones (like cortisol, aldosterone), not epinephrine/norepinephrine.

questions.explanation: Hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine are secreted from the adrenal medulla. They are emergency hormones released in conditions of stress, emergency, etc. Epinephrine and norepinephrine are also released by adrenergic nerve fibres of the sympathetic nervous system, where they act as neurotransmitters.

questions.text: A man has to face an interview, but during his first five minutes before the interview, he experiences sweating, increased heart rate, and respiration. Which hormone is responsible for his restlessness?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Adrenocorticotropic Hormone (ACTH) is involved in regulating cortisol levels, not directly related to 'fight-or-flight' response symptoms.
Insulin and glucagon regulate blood sugar levels, not typically associated with the physical symptoms described.
Epinephrine and norepinephrine are responsible for the 'fight-or-flight' response, causing symptoms like increased heart rate and respiration. This is the correct option.
Aldosterone regulates salt and water balance, not directly related to the physical symptoms described.

questions.explanation: Epinephrine and norepinephrine are responsible for the physical symptoms described because they trigger the 'fight-or-flight' response in stressful situations. These hormones increase heart rate, respiration, and other responses to prepare the body for action. The other options, such as ACTH, insulin, glucagon, and aldosterone, do not directly cause the specific symptoms mentioned in the question.

questions.text: ADH most directly affects:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: ADH does not directly affect the glomerulus. The glomerulus is primarily involved in filtering blood to form urine, but ADH acts further along in the nephron.
ADH does not directly affect Bowman's capsule. This structure is where filtration of blood occurs, preceding where ADH takes action.
ADH does not affect the thymus. The thymus is part of the immune system, not directly involved in water balance or kidney function.
ADH acts directly on the collecting tubules of the nephron, increasing water reabsorption and concentrating the urine.

questions.explanation: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH) is a chemical produced in the brain that causes the kidneys to release less water, decreasing the amount of urine produced. A high ADH level causes the body to produce less urine.

questions.text: All of the following are correct about the endocrine system except:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is true. Endocrine glands release hormones directly into the bloodstream as they do not have ducts.

options.text: Parathormone is the flight or fight hormone
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Parathormone, or parathyroid hormone, primarily regulates calcium levels in the blood, not involved in the fight or flight response.

options.text: The islet cells in the pancreas produce both glucagon and insulin
options.explanation: This statement is true. The islets of Langerhans in the pancreas produce glucagon and insulin to regulate blood sugar levels.

options.text: Ecdysone controls metamorphosis in some insects
options.explanation: This statement is true. Ecdysone is a hormone that plays a critical role in insect development and metamorphosis.

questions.explanation: Adrenaline and noradrenaline are dubbed emergency hormones or Fight or Flight hormones. These hormones are rapidly released in reaction to stress of any kind and during emergency situations.

questions.text: Which would occur if the parasympathetic system were stimulated
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: An increase in blood sugar is generally associated with the sympathetic nervous system, which prepares the body for 'fight or flight' by mobilizing energy.
The parasympathetic nervous system enhances digestion as part of its 'rest and digest' function, conserving energy and promoting bodily functions like digestion and urination.
An increase in breathing rate is linked with the sympathetic nervous system, which increases oxygen intake during stress or activity.
Adrenaline release is a response of the sympathetic nervous system, which prepares the body for immediate physical activity.

questions.explanation: The parasympathetic nervous system predominates in quiet “rest and digest” conditions while the sympathetic nervous system drives the “fight or flight” response in stressful situations. The main purpose of the PNS is to conserve energy to be used later and to regulate bodily functions like digestion and urination.

questions.text: The deficiency of adrenal cortex hormones can lead to _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Dwarfism is primarily related to a deficiency of growth hormone, not adrenal cortex hormones.
GERD (Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease) is influenced by factors such as diet and lifestyle, and is not directly related to adrenal cortex hormone deficiency.
Addison's Disease is caused by a deficiency in adrenal cortex hormones, leading to symptoms like fatigue, muscle weakness, and weight loss.
Acromegaly is caused by excessive production of growth hormone, not a deficiency in adrenal cortex hormones.

questions.explanation: When adrenal glands don't produce enough hormones, this can lead to adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease). Adrenal glands may develop nodules that can be benign or malignant, which can potentially produce excessive amounts of certain hormones leading to various health issues.

questions.text: Which of the following are not steroid hormones but still do not act through second messenger system, are small, nonpolar and can be taken orally
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Prostaglandins are regulatory molecules but are not classified as hormones and do not fit the criteria of being orally active non-steroid hormones.
While estrogen and progesterone are nonpolar and can be taken orally, they are steroid hormones, which disqualifies them based on the question criteria.
Insulin and glucagon are peptide hormones, which are polar and not suitable for oral consumption as they would be degraded in the digestive system.
Thyroid hormones (T3 and T4) are non-steroid hormones that are nonpolar, can cross membranes without second messengers, and can be effectively taken orally, making them the correct choice.

questions.explanation: Prostaglandins are regulatory molecules but are not considered hormones. Estrogens and progesterones are nonpolar but are steroids. Insulin and glucagon are polar therefore cannot be taken orally.
hints: do not act through second messenger system

questions.text: Whether a molecule acts as a neurotransmitter or a hormone, which statement about physiological regulation by these molecules is false?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Target cells must have specific receptor proteins with which these molecules combine
options.explanation: This statement is true because both neurotransmitters and hormones require specific receptors on target cells to elicit a physiological response.

options.text: When these molecules bind to target cells, specific sequences of changes must occur
options.explanation: This statement is true as binding to target cells triggers a cascade of cellular events leading to a physiological response.

options.text: There must be an 'off-switch' that will stop the induced changes
options.explanation: This statement is true since regulatory mechanisms are in place to terminate the actions of these molecules after their effects are achieved.

options.text: Second messengers will always be involved in the response of the target cell
options.explanation: This statement is false. Second messengers are typically involved in the response to polar molecules, while nonpolar molecules can directly affect intracellular processes without them.

questions.explanation: This statement is false because not all hormones and neurotransmitters utilize second messengers. Steroid hormones (like testosterone and estrogen) and thyroid hormones are lipid-soluble, allowing them to pass directly through the cell membrane and bind to intracellular receptors in the cytoplasm or nucleus, directly influencing gene expression without needing a second messenger. Conversely, protein/peptide hormones and most neurotransmitters are water-soluble and must bind to surface receptors, which then typically trigger a second messenger cascade inside the cell to transmit the signal.

questions.text: Between which of the following hormone pairs is the interaction between hormones not an example of the permissive effect of a first hormone for a second hormone?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Estrogen for prolactin on the mammary glands during pregnancy
options.explanation: Estrogen stimulates the pituitary gland, leading to elevated levels of prolactin, which in turn prepares the mammary glands for milk production. This is an example of a permissive effect.

options.text: Parathyroid hormone for vitamin D3 on blood Ca2+ levels
options.explanation: Parathyroid hormone enhances the effect of vitamin D3 by promoting calcium absorption in the gut, crucial for maintaining blood calcium levels. This is a permissive interaction.

options.text: Estrogen for progesterone on the uterus
options.explanation: Estrogen prepares the uterine lining, making it receptive to progesterone, which then maintains the uterine environment for pregnancy. This is also a permissive effect.

options.text: Insulin for glucagon on adipose tissue
options.explanation: Insulin and glucagon have opposing effects on adipose tissue, where insulin promotes fat storage while glucagon promotes fat breakdown. This represents an antagonistic interaction, not permissive.

questions.explanation: The action of insulin and glucagon on adipose tissue is an example of antagonistic effect. Insulin decreases blood sugar level whereas glucagon increases it.

questions.text: Glucocorticoids do not:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Glucocorticoids enhance gluconeogenesis, which is the process of generating glucose from non-carbohydrate sources.
Glucocorticoids promote lipolysis, which is the breakdown of lipids to release fatty acids.
Glucocorticoids facilitate proteolysis, the breakdown of proteins into amino acids.

options.text: Stimulate cellular uptake and utilization of amino acids
options.explanation: Glucocorticoids actually inhibit the cellular uptake and utilization of amino acids, rather than stimulating it.
